content.problemStatement:
Alice and Bob are visiting a shop with NNN different items arranged in a row. The shop is represented by a permutation PPP of the numbers [1,2,…,N][1, 2, \ldots, N][1,2,…,N], where PiP_iPi​ denotes the price of the iii-th item from the left. There is an **infinite supply** of each item.

Both Alice and Bob have KKK coins.


Alice starts from the **left** and moves right. At each item, if she can afford it and hasn't bought it yet, she buys **one unit** of that item and reduces her coins accordingly. She continues until she runs out of coins or reaches the end.
Bob does the same, but he starts from the **right** and moves left.
Define f(P)f(P)f(P) to be the number of integers KKK such that 2≤K≤N2 \le K \le N2≤K≤N and the set of items purchased by Alice is equal to the set of items purchased by Bob (note that order doesn't matter).

You are given a partially filled permutation PPP of length NNN, where some positions are marked as −1-1−1.

Compute the sum of f(P)f(P)f(P) over all valid ways to fill the permutation (i.e., all ways to replace the −1-1−1 entries with numbers from [1,2,…,N][1, 2, \ldots, N][1,2,…,N] to form a valid permutation).

As the answer can be large, output it modulo 998244353998244353998244353.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the number of items in the shop.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers — the partially filled permutation P1,P2,…,PNP_1, P_2, \ldots, P_NP1​,P2​,…,PN​, where each value is either −1-1−1 or an integer in [1,N][1, N][1,N].

It's guaranteed that the values not equal to −1-1−1 are distinct.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the sum of f(P)f(P)f(P) over all valid ways to fill the permutation, modulo 998244353998244353998244353.
constraints:
∀1≤i<j≤N orall 1 \leq i \lt j \leq N∀1≤i<j≤N, Pi≠−1 and Pj≠−1  ⟹  Pi≠PjP_i 
e -1 	ext{ and } P_j 
e -1 \implies P_i 
e P_jPi​=−1 and Pj​=−1⟹Pi​=Pj​
The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 222: The only possible permutation is P=[1,3,2]P = [1, 3, 2]P=[1,3,2].
This has f(P)=1f(P) = 1f(P)=1, because:

For K=2K = 2K=2, Alice will buy the item with price 111 while Bob will buy the item with price 222.
For K=3K = 3K=3, Alice and Bob will both buy the items with costs 111 and 222.

Test case 333: P=[1,2,3]P = [1, 2, 3]P=[1,2,3] is fixed.

For K=2K = 2K=2, Alice will buy {1}\{1\}{1} and Bob will buy {2}\{2\}{2}.
For K=3K = 3K=3, Alice will buy {1,2}\{1, 2\}{1,2} and Bob will buy {3}\{3\}{3}.

In either case the sets are not equal, so f(P)=0f(P) = 0f(P)=0.
